title: Corchea: Paper-Based Microfluidic Device
author: Gonzalo Vidal-Peña
license_note: Confirmed via CrossRef DOI metadata: dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.vtwe6pe

steps.title: Draw the borders of your device...
steps.instruction: Draw the borders of your device and the future channels in your paper (#1 Whatman)

steps.title: Fill with wax crayons all the...
steps.instruction: Fill with wax crayons all the parts that you dont want the fluid pass

steps.title: Melt the wax, we use a...
steps.instruction: Melt the wax, we use a regular oven at 100 °C for 140s. Remove it from the heat and let the wax solidify. It must cross the paper.

steps.title: Put the paper in between the acrylic sheets
steps.instruction: Put the paper in between the acrylic sheets. You can put some tape to make it more impermable.

steps.title: Put the acrylic paper sandwich in...
steps.instruction: Put the acrylic paper sandwich in the lid of the hardware.

steps.title: Introduce the paper ends in the...
steps.instruction: Introduce the paper ends in the Source and close the device. You can use a tip box as pint of support.

steps.title: Insert the paper acrylic sandwich in...
steps.instruction: Insert the paper acrylic sandwich in the clip to keep pressure in it and put the filter. Now you are ready to do experiments.

steps.title: Put your favorite bacteria direct in...
steps.instruction: Put your favorite bacteria direct in the channels or you can use paper discs previusly cultured with bacteria.

steps.title: Set your experiment
steps.instruction: Set your experiment. In this case we introduce the device in an incubator and get results with FluoPi a Low-Cost and Open-Acces transiluminator. Fabricate your own FluoPi https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0187163
